article.title: U.S. Government Entity Paid Kairos $1 Million in Data-Theft Extortion Case
article.description: A U.S. government entity paid about $1 million to keep stolen files from being leaked, according to a new case study by Rakesh Krishnan for Ransom-ISAC, built on a leaked negotiation chat and the blockchain trail the payment left. The odd part: the group that took the money calls itself Kairos, but it may not be a ransomware gang at all. Krishnan found no sign that it ever locked a single

generated.caption:
Imagine your government paying $1 MILLION just to keep stolen data private... 🤯

That's exactly what a U.S. government entity recently did! A new case study by Rakesh Krishnan for Ransom-ISAC revealed they paid around $1 million to prevent confidential files from being leaked online.

The group behind this? 'Kairos.' But here's the twist: Kairos isn't your typical ransomware gang. Researchers found no evidence that they encrypted any files. Instead, they focused solely on *stealing* data and demanding payment to stop its public release – a pure data-theft extortion play.

This unique incident highlights a growing trend where attackers skip the encryption step entirely, focusing directly on the value of sensitive information and the fear of exposure. The case study used leaked negotiation chats and blockchain payment trails to uncover the details.

Stay informed about evolving cyber threats!
👉 Save this post to remember this critical insight.
👉 Share it with someone who needs to know.

slides.text: US Gov Paid $1M in Data-Theft Extortion

slides.heading: The Extortion Case
slides.text: A US government entity paid $1 million to prevent stolen files from being leaked, according to a case study by Rakesh Krishnan.

slides.heading: The Perpetrators
slides.text: The group behind the extortion calls itself Kairos, but it may not be a traditional ransomware gang, as no evidence of file locking was found.

slides.heading: Investigation Findings
slides.text: The case study is based on a leaked negotiation chat and a blockchain trail left by the payment, which totals around $1 million.

slides.heading: Uncertainty Surrounds Kairos
slides.text: Researchers found no sign that Kairos has ever engaged in typical ransomware activities, such as locking files, raising questions about their true intentions.
Stay ahead of threats like Kairos - follow us for the latest cybersecurity updates and alerts!
